skill.name: postmortem-writing
skill.description: Write effective blameless postmortems with root cause analysis, timelines, and action items
skill.summary: Create blameless postmortems that drive organizational learning and prevent incident recurrence
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 34 patterns that are all false positives. The backtick patterns are Markdown code block delimiters, not shell command execution. The URLs are reference links to external documentation, not network calls. This skill contains only documentation and templates for writing postmortems.

content.value_statement: Turn incidents into organizational learning without blame. Create structured postmortems that identify root causes and prevent recurrence.
actual_capabilities: Generate structured postmortem documents with executive summary, timeline, and root cause analysis
Facilitate 5 Whys analysis to identify underlying causes
Create actionable follow-up items with owners and due dates
Provide templates for SEV1/SEV2 incidents and quick postmortems
Guide blameless culture during incident reviews
Document detection gaps and response effectiveness
limitations: Requires human input for incident facts and timeline details
Cannot access your monitoring systems or incident data directly
Templates may need customization for your organization's processes
Does not replace formal incident management tooling

use_cases.title: Post-Incident Review
use_cases.description: After resolving a production incident, use this skill to structure the postmortem document with timeline, impact assessment, and root cause analysis.

use_cases.title: Incident Response Training
use_cases.description: Use the templates and guides to train new team members on effective incident documentation and blameless review practices.

use_cases.title: Organizational Learning
use_cases.description: Create standardized postmortems that can be shared across teams to prevent similar incidents and spread learnings.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Postmortem Creation
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a postmortem for an incident that occurred on [DATE]. The service [SERVICE NAME] was down for [DURATION] affecting [NUMBER] users. The issue was caused by [BRIEF DESCRIPTION]. Generate a postmortem document with executive summary, timeline, and initial root cause analysis.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need to quickly draft a postmortem after an incident

prompt_templates.title: 5 Whys Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Conduct a 5 Whys analysis for this incident: [INCIDENT DESCRIPTION]. Start with the problem statement and drill down through at least 5 levels of why questions to identify root causes.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need to identify underlying causes beyond surface symptoms

prompt_templates.title: Postmortem Meeting Facilitation
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm facilitating a postmortem meeting for [INCIDENT SUMMARY]. Create a meeting agenda with time boxes, key discussion questions, and tips for maintaining a blameless environment during the review.
prompt_templates.scenario: You are leading a postmortem meeting and need a structured agenda

prompt_templates.title: Action Item Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Based on this incident analysis [PASTE ANALYSIS], generate prioritized action items. For each item include: priority level, specific action, recommended owner role, estimated timeline, and whether it prevents, detects, or mitigates future occurrences.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have identified root causes and need concrete follow-up tasks

output_examples.input: Help me write a postmortem. Payment service was down for 47 minutes after deployment v2.3.4. Root cause was database connection pool exhaustion.
output_examples.output: Generates a complete postmortem with: Executive Summary with impact metrics, detailed Timeline table with timestamps, Root Cause Analysis using 5 Whys, Detection section covering what worked and gaps, Response evaluation, Impact assessment (customers, business, technical), Lessons Learned, and Action Items table with priorities and owners

output_examples.input: Conduct 5 Whys for: API latency spiked after cache flush
output_examples.output: Provides structured 5 Whys analysis starting with problem statement, then drilling through each why with evidence, identifying root causes (missing cache warming, no partial invalidation), and systemic improvements with prevention and detection strategies

best_practices: Start the postmortem within 24 hours while memories are fresh
Focus on what conditions allowed the failure, not who made mistakes
Assign every action item to a specific owner with a due date
anti_patterns: Naming individuals as the cause of the incident
Creating action items without owners or deadlines
Skipping postmortems for minor incidents that reveal patterns

faq.question: What incidents require a postmortem?
faq.answer: Postmortems are recommended for SEV1/SEV2 incidents, customer-facing outages over 15 minutes, data loss or security incidents, near-misses with high severity potential, and novel failure modes that reveal new risks.

faq.question: How do I maintain a blameless culture?
faq.answer: Focus questions on what conditions allowed the failure rather than who made mistakes. Use neutral language. Remember that people make mistakes because systems allow them. The goal is learning, not punishment.

faq.question: When should the postmortem meeting be held?
faq.answer: Schedule the meeting 3-5 days after the incident. This allows time to draft the document and gather facts, while memories are still fresh. The meeting typically takes 60 minutes.

faq.question: Who should attend the postmortem meeting?
faq.answer: Include everyone involved in the incident response: on-call engineers, incident commander, relevant service owners, and stakeholders. Keep the group small enough for productive discussion, typically 8-12 people.

faq.question: What makes a good action item?
faq.answer: Good action items are specific, achievable, and tracked. They have a clear owner, due date, and ticket reference. Focus on systemic improvements rather than one-off fixes. Prioritize by impact and effort.

faq.question: Should we postmortem near-misses?
faq.answer: Yes. Near-misses that could have caused severe incidents often reveal the same systemic issues as actual incidents. They provide learning opportunities without the pressure of an actual outage.
